name: Argon
name_origin: From the Greek 'argos' meaning 'lazy' or 'idle', reflecting its chemical inertness.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Argon is the third most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere at about 0.934% by volume, after nitrogen and oxygen. Most of the argon on Earth (Ar-40) is produced by radioactive decay of potassium-40 in the crust.
states: trace amounts dissolved in seawater and groundwater

compounds.name: Argon fluorohydride
compounds.summary: The first stable neutral compound of argon, observed at very low temperatures (~17 K) in 2000.

compounds.name: Argon clathrate hydrate
compounds.summary: An inclusion compound where argon atoms are trapped in a cage-like water lattice at low temperature and elevated pressure.

compounds.name: Argonium ion
compounds.summary: A cation of argon detected in interstellar space, notably in the Crab Nebula; the first noble-gas molecule found in space.

compounds.name: Argon excimer
compounds.summary: An excited-state diatomic argon used to generate UV light around 126 nm in excimer lamps and lasers.

compounds.name: Argon-metal cation complexes
compounds.summary: Weakly bound gas-phase ions in which argon coordinates to transition-metal cations, observed by mass spectrometry.

uses: Inert shielding gas for arc welding (e.g., MIG and TIG processes) of stainless steel and aluminum
Fill gas in incandescent and fluorescent light bulbs to prevent filament oxidation
Inert atmosphere for growing silicon and germanium crystals in semiconductor manufacturing
Insulating gas fill between panes of energy-efficient double- and triple-glazed windows
Carrier and plasma gas in inductively coupled plasma (ICP) and gas chromatography instruments
Cryogenic liquid in some laboratory and medical applications, including argon-laser surgery
reactivity_summary: Argon is a colorless, odorless monatomic noble gas with a complete valence electron shell, making it chemically inert under nearly all conditions. Only a handful of metastable compounds (such as HArF) and weakly bound complexes have ever been observed.
isotopes_summary: Argon has three stable isotopes: Ar-40 (~99.6% on Earth, but only ~84% in the Sun), Ar-36 (~0.33%), and Ar-38 (~0.06%). The terrestrial dominance of Ar-40 from K-40 decay underlies the K-Ar and Ar-Ar dating methods used in geology.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Argon
